risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The participants were randomised with a computer-generated randomisation list stratified by centre... The allocation code, including details of block size (10), was not revealed to the researchers or the clinicians until recruitment and data collection were complete." Comment: A computer-generated randomisation list is a clearly random allocation method, and stratification by centre with concealed block size is consistent with proper random sequence generation.

Quote: "At each centre, the person responsible for inclusion emailed the research unit, presented the participant’s project ID number, name and gender before treatment allocation was disclosed. The allocation code, including details of block size (10), was not revealed to the researchers or the clinicians until recruitment and data collection were complete." Comment: This explicitly indicates that the allocation code was kept hidden from recruiters/clinicians until after recruitment, and participants were allocated only after the research unit disclosed the assignment, which is sufficient to prevent foreknowledge of the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After random allocation, participants were informed about the outcome, and the intervention group was given a date for its first session." Comment: Participants and treating personnel could not have been blinded in this pragmatic psychotherapy/vocational rehabilitation trial; the intervention was clearly distinguishable from usual care, and the main outcomes (work participation and self-reported mental health) could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "Using data from the national social insurance register and the national employee register, we could (with no loss to follow-up) determine if a person (1) was in regular work... The primary outcome was ‘increased or maintained work participation’..." and "The authors in charge of the data analysis were blinded for intervention assignment." Comment: The primary outcome was ascertained from national registers rather than assessor judgement or patient report, so knowledge of allocation would be very unlikely to influence measurement. Although the paper does not explicitly say the outcome assessor was blinded, the objective registry-based outcome and blinded data analysts make detection bias for the primary outcome unlikely.

Quote: "Data on the main outcome measure, work participation, were complete for all participants. However, for secondary outcomes based on self-report, 740 (62%) participants at 6 months follow-up and 636 (52%) participants at 12 months follow-up returned their questionnaires" Comment: The primary outcome had no missing data because it was obtained from national registers with no loss to follow-up, so attrition is unlikely to bias the main result. Although self-report secondary outcomes had substantial non-response, the paper used inverse probability weights to address possible attrition bias, and this does not undermine the primary outcome assessment.
